know, realizing you're early on in your journey building out the marketing side of the house, would love to just hear a little bit more kind of like what triggered the conversation kind of like, you know, it would also give us a little bit of perspective on what's driving some of your growth today, what you feel confident in, and we can tailor this in a variety of different ways, but that can be a useful context for us.

How do we, you know, how do we approach ranking for both of those?
How do we measure them?
What's the right thing to do?
So, you know, I'm focused on a number of things.

And, you every capability needs a web page.
Every use case needs a web page.
And, you know, right now, it's more just like, you know, homepage, platform page, company.
You know, so we obviously need to figure out, like, what terms are we targeting?
You know, where do we want to rank?
You know, is there anything that we want to double down on the blog?
Do we need a knowledge center that's sort of with articles that's separate from the blog, separate from the product pages?
So that's kind of what, you know, what we're looking into.
So I need to figure out sort of what's the best approach.
Awesome.

And Kyle, I can jump in here if you're looking at it, because I think I have so much context in this space as well, and it could be helpful, but maybe if it makes sense, I can just give you a super quick on kind of how we work with companies, the types of companies we work with, and then just show you like a bunch of stuff, because it's usually a lot better than...
One quick question before we do that, as Marcel's pulling some of that up.
Are you all doing like outbound today?
You know, you got to Series A, so I imagine you're getting some traction somewhere, you know, as you're starting to build this inbound engine.
So are you largely referral outbound based at this point?
Yeah, exactly.
We have kind of a very strong network of security leaders that we work with, and...
We are running some LinkedIn ads, you know, minimal on Google.

So, you know, we have some inbound going, but yes, you know, it's more, you know, itamar network and, you know, a couple of sellers are aggressive and, you know, we'll explore, excuse me, you know, SDRs and all that.
But yes, doing more, being more findable, certainly critical for us.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And yeah, super familiar.

I'll tell you a little bit about like kind of the journey, won't go into my background too much, but I think what's relevant here is early on at IBM, I had built a site called securityintelligence.com.
And it recently got folded back into IBM.com, but it was the highest ROI marketing program in that division.
And it lasted over 50 years, and now I got folded back into IBM.com, but that's kind of how things got started.
And then for us.
It was the same thing.
It was always in your shoes, Russell, where I'm coming in, and it was kind of the same thing every company I've ever worked for.
It's like, okay, we're spending on ads or outbound or other initiatives.
How do we grow our organic engine?
It was always like, okay, let me go hire an editor that knows my space well.
Then let me go hire some writers and contributors.
Then let start cranking content.
Then let figure out how to get it on the website.
And then let me figure out how to get backlinks.
Let me figure out how to grow that, build some distribution, you know, over and over again.
And it never felt kind of a little bit easier.

And so then when I was at DeepGram, I started just taking a lot of what I had learned over the years and just building workflows to enable us to just do those things better, faster, you know, and cheaper.
And so after building these workflows, I started hiring the editors, the experts, and putting them in the loop and then, like, just cranking high-quality content for a while.
And we just saw this kind of insane growth and, you know, 4X revenue.
And from there, we

Started doing a lot of these kind of workshops, teaching people how to do it, and then that's what led to GrowthX, where ultimately we started working with a lot of different customers in different industries where we thought they wanted the automation only and the workflows, but ultimately they also needed help with the strategy and the execution of the strategy as well.

And so we kind of do those three layers, right, which is the strategy, the AI workflows to argument a lot of how we want to go about achieving that quality and scale, and then the humans in the loop to also like QA, edit, and make sure like we're publishing, optimizing, and it'll make more sense.
I'll show you some examples like of normal security that we work with and a few others, you know, so.

But actually one quick thing, just for context, so yeah, I talked to a couple, you know, SEO agencies, you know, more traditional, like I mentioned, you know, I had done the, you know, HubSpot Academy, you know, SEO course a couple of times over the years.
We did, we had.
I've talked to a company called Daydream, sort of, you know, I don't know if they're a direct competitor, but just sort of give you a sense.
And if you could sort of talk through, as you're talking through this, if you have differences in approach, that would be helpful to understand.
Yeah, absolutely.
And so I think that the main thing in kind of how we're approaching things is just how we're kind of set up and the tech behind it, right?
Like, so the way we're set up today is we have managing directors that oversee all of the engagements, right?
And we also have the chief content officer, Matthew Pinserino, who was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for 10 years.
Super, super versed in tech as well.
But, and then our managing directors are people that have managed massive publications.
So, like, Daryl, for example, used to oversee Apple's blog, and then he was at a comp for Shopify.
You know, like, there's, you know, folks on the team who oversaw, like, SEO growth for companies like TripAdvisor, and we have kind of a variety of kind of these.
And then under them, we have managing editors and content managers that are embedded within your team, essentially, right?
But the main kind of way we approach things is we're going through this, what we call strategy sprint with you.
And what we're doing in the strategy sprint is calibrating your strategy and calibrating kind of the voice you want to have, the tone.
What is your definition of quality?
What's important to you?
And then customizing all our workflows.
But then in addition to that, we're also building a content OS for you, right?
So right now, we're actually just in the process of moving a lot of this to our own platform.
But I'll just show you kind of how we approach things.
So let's just take like abnormal security, for example, where we looked at a lot of different kind of opportunities that they can go after.
So take, for example, I would.
We came up with over 1,000 potential assignments totaling 41 million monthly searches, right?
And then we created all these clusters based on the different topics after talking to them.
So it's not just like creating variables, templatizing a page, and hitting go, right?
We think like you need a lot more strategy behind that before you go and you're ready to go do 5,000 pages.
And so that's kind of the main difference, I think, is a lot of what Daydream seems to be really good.
And a lot of what they do is programmatic.
And we do have programmatic engagements as well.

But companies like you with very little domain authority, not a lot of pages published, not a lot of backlinks, you know, we highly, highly not recommend you going and publishing 1,000 pages programmatically.
You know, like you want to start with editorial, which is similar to what we're doing for abnormal security.
We're just signing up Sentinel-1, Okta, you know, so we're like fairly familiar.
With, like, the security audience, and we know, like, it's a tricky one as well, right?

But the main thing that we're doing is coming up with these opportunities based on the context of what we know about you, right, and based on the calibration, and then starting to produce the content for you, right?

And so this is everything from, like, not only the workflows that I'll show you here in a second, but everything to get it live, whether it's, like, graphics, whether it's optimization, whether it's publishing in the CMS.
So we're your publishing team.
And then how you interact with that, how much you're hands-on is really up to you, right?
So if you're, you know, if you want to go in here and be more hands-on and provide more comments and whatnot, great.
But usually, like, what most customers end up doing is, like, they'll maybe, like, review a couple of these and then calibrate it.
But then over time, we'll more be in the place where they're kind of reviewing what to prioritize and giving us directions on.
I'm like, hey, based on where the business is going, these are the topics we care more about, you know, emphasize a bit more on that front, right?
And then behind the scenes, like what we build is a workflow engine.
And so what this is, is like every single one of our processes are powered by our AI workflows.
And we have a coding agent that essentially builds these workflows and a runtime layer that runs this.
so what that means is, you know, anything we're doing, so let's say like generating context on your company or doing deep research, I'll show you the deep researcher one on that topic for you.
Like it's all like a very, very complex workflow that goes and fetches a bunch of information, but all with the context of your company, right?
And then we have the humans in the loop.
doing that.
So if you look here, for example, like, just to show you really quickly, this one, let's see, Strappy and 0.0.

data, and it's generating a bunch of research questions to go research, and then it's fetching several different sources from different APIs, it's scraping those citations, and then reviewing if those citations are relevant to you or not, right?
And then combining those results.
And so what that ends up looking like on the production side is something kind of like this, where it's going through several different workflows, right?

And then you have your editors that are assigned to you reviewing those workflows, and editing, and guiding it, and figuring out the right plan to go execute, to write the best piece of content for you, optimizing it, fact-checking all of the above, all the way to hitting publish on your site, staging it, and all that, right?
But also any refresh content as well.
And so that's a little bit of the TLDR there, and then I'll just show you a quick example here with, like, let me just pull Slack so you can see, like, this is Galileo AI, one of our customers.

And you can see, like, we're reporting weekly, we're providing updates, we're interacting, we're publishing, figuring out the right signals, doubling down on what's working, and then working together, you know, and being embedded in your team.
so in this case, Mara is the director, Vivek is the managing editor, Usman and a few others are the technical editors on the account, right?
Like, and so we're essentially like a full staff agency to do content, but powered by a lot of AI workflows.
Good, good.
So we're thinking about the content that you're then creating.
So are those like, you know, do you have like a hub and spoke model for assets?
Is it sort of you have a topic and then it's a bunch of different things in different styles that, how would you structure those on the site?
Is it like, you know, an article section would then like, you know, you know, will then have sort of hundreds of things there?
Like, how do you, how would that end up looking?
Okay.
It really depends from client to client.
So like right now you have a blog, right?
And so you normally like the starting point is let's not go create a new section on your site before even calibrating, right?
And first we need to figure out what does great look like for you?
And like, what's your brand voice?
What do you want to say?
How do you want to say it?
What are the trusted sources we should go fetch for content, right?
Like how do we achieve that?
And so that's part of the calibration and the strategy sprint.
Once we achieve that, then we can figure out, hey, does it make sense to go scale the hell out of this and go do like really high volume things?
Does it make sense to be more, you know, more thoughtful about the bets we place down?

And for most customers, like with Abnormal who has a massive footprint on their website and thousands of pages, like we pick some of the clusters and topic pillars, if you will, that we want to go after initially, right?
And we try to narrow and start with at least one persona.
So if you come and you say, hey, you know what, like I need a, you know, a super
Needy-greedy IT security person, like persona.
But then we also want to talk to CISAs at the same time.
And we also want to do this.
Like, we'll probably tell you, like, hey, you're super early on.
You have very little domain authority.
You haven't published enough content.
I wouldn't spread yourself something like that from the beginning, right?
Let's pick one.
Let's pick three lanes that are topics of lanes.
Let's place some bets down.
Let's learn from that and then iterate very quickly and look at it weekly.
So that's normally how we start.
But then if you look at Okta or Auth0 or Sentinel-1 or Abnormal, like, we try to parallelize with things like, hey, you have 1,000 pages a year that haven't been touched in four years.
We should probably go fix that really quickly as well.
So then we might run multiple paths in parallel, you know, with them.
Got it.
You know, that makes sense.
I think, you know, my concern with something like Daydream was just, you know, if on, like, you know, day 10, we all of a sudden have, like, 300 pages on the website.
It seems like that's sort of unnatural enough that there will be negative consequences at some point, if not right away.
You know, I feel like, you know, we've seen competitors do some similar things and all of a sudden have, you know, posts, articles published elsewhere, you know, all over the place.
But like, and, you know, maybe there's a role for that.
But like you said, like right off the bat, you know, going from like, you know, a couple dozen pages on the website to hundreds, it seems like you want to do that with some, some care behind it.
Yeah, so it's one of our customers, Tiro, they're obviously not in the technical space, but just to give you an idea of like, the, the kind of the, the approach there.
They didn't even have a blog when we started with them.
So 100% of their blog content was us.
And so like, we're trying to be very, very thoughtful with that.
We didn't start by publishing 20, we started by calibrating one, and then publishing two, and then three, and then learning, and then daily, getting to daily publishing.
For us, like, what is most important is building, reaching quality, picking the right opportunities.

Optimizing for taking the best shots on goal and not taking the most shots on goal, especially in your instance where you don't have a crazy domain authority, and then publishing daily or refreshing daily, right?
And so essentially constantly giving Google the signals and other NLMs the signals.
There's pressure stuff here every time you come back, and we are adding value daily to our site.
And so it's like incremental.
It's like improving by 1% daily versus trying to throw 1,000 things over there that are low-quality and bad signals, right?
So I'll give you like a very specific like example here, like with VACI, which we are doing more programmatic where we're building these voice agent pages for them.
And so we built 100 of these.
We're not doing 1,000.
Like we spent three, four weeks figuring out the page, doing the design, you know, building it, and then getting the right content in place, right?
Which for you, I wouldn't start there.
would start with editorial because it's a low-hanging fruit for you.
But then we're being actually very thoughtful to me.
Make sure that as we're publishing this, that it's actually like being calibrated, right?
And so like we're looking at signals and I'll just, I'll show you really quickly here.
But essentially like you look at this and you say, and I'm going a little bit more in the weeds here, but super quickly.
Okay, so it's only been like less than a week, right?
But you can see like there's 98 clicks and almost 6,000 impressions.
But if you look at the click-through rate, that's pretty insane.
That's like almost a 6% click-through rate of those impressions, right?
And we publish 100 pages and almost every page we publish now is getting impressions and clicks, right?
So now we have high confidence because they all got indexed that we can go in the next batch, you know?
So that's what we mean when we say like let's, you know, plant high-quality seeds on, you know, the best shots on goal with the right opportunities, learn from
After we go through the calibration, we feel content about the quality, and then just learn daily and publish daily.
And if we learn daily and publish daily, and the quality is good, I've never not seen good signals in the long term.
Short term, there might be ups and downs, but long term.
So what's the pricing model?
Yeah, so we're very transparent with our pricing.
Essentially, the way it works is we do a strategy sprint, which is $20,000 in eight weeks.

In that strategy sprint, we're giving you an entire, like, OS of, like, essentially, like, even if you decide you don't want to work with us, we're doing all this, like, you know, keyword analysis, figuring out all every opportunity, looking at all your competitors, and starting to create assignments for you, right?

Like, and then helping to vet all of this, we're creating all the artifacts, the style guides, everything you need to essentially start publishing, and we start to publish as quickly as we calibrate, right?
So we try to calibrate as quickly as possible and start to publish.
So eight weeks is just an opportunity for us to say, hey, is this working?
Like, do we want to work together and for you to do the same?
And for us to set the right strategy, essentially, right?
And also understand what is your definition of done?
Like, if you look at abnormal security, their definition of done is very different from Tiro, which is in, you know, a very different space, right?
So we want to calibrate that versus just trying to go to publishing.
And then after that, we go into what's called growth execution.
Most of our engagements in that phase are about $12,000 to $15,000 a month on the editorial front.
So just to give you kind of like the ballpark, right?
But that's really like usually calibrated during that first eight weeks, right?
And so, but it defaults into a growth execution.
And that's when we're just like working together, publishing daily and doing all of that.
So the first eight weeks is where you will get the whole strategy, everything else almost to execute, and it's no risk.
And we're usually like booked out about six to eight weeks.
And so, like, we're booking right now about mid to late July right now for kickoffs on that.
And it's just because, like, it's so low risk and we're so capacity constrained due to demand that then that just makes it easier and you get a ton of value in that process no matter what, you know.
Got it.
No, I think this definitely sounds interesting.
I think we just need to discuss what next steps would be.
If you could, I guess, just, you know, send over what the package is just so I don't forget, that would be great.
And that's I can discuss it.
Mark, do you have any other questions?
No, that was really thorough and sounds interesting.
Thanks a lot.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
So, Khan team will follow up and then we'll add you all to a shared Slack if you're open to it, just so that we can respond to questions a lot faster and share more things with you.
And then we'll send a recording of this and then send you, like, kind of like a.
sample proposal with some proposed dates that are open if you do decide you want to kick off, you know, just to kind of pencil in if you decide to move forward.
That sounds great, sounds good.
Perfect.
Thank you so much.
Great, great connecting.
All right, yeah, good talking to you as well.
right, take care.
Nice to meet you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introductory call between Token Security (Itamar/Russ) and GrowthX (Marcel) to discuss potential collaboration on content marketing and SEO strategies.](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Token Security is a Series A startup in the non-human identity (NHI) security space, seeking to improve their content marketing and SEO presence](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [GrowthX offers a comprehensive content strategy and execution service, combining AI-powered workflows with human expertise](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [GrowthX's approach emphasizes quality over quantity, starting with editorial content and gradually scaling up based on performance and learnings](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Pricing model: $20k for 8-week strategy sprint, followed by $12k-$15k/month for growth execution](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Topics

### Token Security's Current Situation and Needs

  - [Series A startup with 35 employees, raised $20M in January](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)
  - [In the hot non-human identity (NHI) security space](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=297.0)
  - [Current marketing efforts:](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
      - [Primarily relying on network-based outbound and referrals](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
      - [Running some LinkedIn ads, minimal Google ads](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=542.0)
      - [Website lacks content; behind in SEO rankings and domain authority](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=401.0)
  - [Seeking to improve findability and organic growth](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [Russ (VP of Marketing) exploring options like SEMrush, Ahrefs, and traditional agencies](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=706.0)

### GrowthX's Approach and Offerings

  - [Combines AI-powered workflows with human expertise](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=735.0)
  - [Team structure:](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=750.0)
      - [Managing directors oversee engagements](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=750.0)
      - [Chief Content Officer (former TechCrunch editor-in-chief)](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=750.0)
      - [Managing editors and content managers embedded in client teams](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=750.0)
  - [Process:](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=797.0)
    1.  Strategy sprint: Calibrate strategy, voice, tone, and quality definition
    2.  Build custom content OS for client
    3.  Create content clusters and topic opportunities
    4.  Produce, optimize, and publish content
    5.  Continuous learning and iteration
  - [AI-powered workflow engine automates research and content creation processes](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
  - [Human editors review, edit, and ensure quality](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1034.0)

### Content Strategy and Execution

  - [Tailored approach based on client's current situation and goals](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=735.0)
  - [For new sites with low domain authority:](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1207.0)
      - [Start with editorial content, not programmatic](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1207.0)
      - [Focus on 1-3 topic lanes and a single persona initially](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
      - [Gradually increase publishing frequency](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1313.0)
      - [Optimize existing content in parallel](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1425.0)
  - [Emphasis on daily publishing and refreshing to provide consistent signals to search engines](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1446.0)
  - [Regular reporting and collaboration with client team](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1456.0)

### Pricing and Engagement Model

  - [Strategy sprint: $20,000 for 8 weeks](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1458.0)
      - [Includes comprehensive strategy, keyword analysis, competitor research, and initial content creation](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1485.0)
      - [Low-risk opportunity to assess fit and set strategy](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1500.0)
  - [Growth execution: $12,000 to $15,000 per month](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1524.0)
      - [Ongoing content creation, optimization, and publishing](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1527.0)
  - [Currently booking 6-8 weeks out (mid to late July for new engagements)](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)

## Next Steps

  - [GrowthX to send over detailed package information and sample proposal](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1605.0)
  - [GrowthX to share recording of the meeting](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1615.0)
  - [GrowthX to propose potential kickoff dates](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)
  - [Token Security team to discuss internally and determine next steps](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1628.0)
  - [Potential addition to shared Slack channel for faster communication](https://fathom.video/share/6tyMnZNapKfTHuAPDtz-EijLCuj9avsi?tab=summary&timestamp=1605.0)
